coredata.dc:title: Acido-treatment of PEDOT:PSS/Carbon Dots (CDots) nano-composite films for high thermoelectric power factor performance and generator
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shamy, Ahmed Gamal
coredata.dc:description:
Thermoelectric materials are considered a main key for sustainable development because they have the ability to convert a low-quality heat into electrical energy. Among them, polymer nano-composite materials have captured attention owing to their unique features such as resilience, low or no toxicity, and abundance. In this work, the thermoelectric parameters of carbon dots/poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulphonate) (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) nano-composite films have been significantly enhanced by using Sulphuric (H2SO4) acids treatment approach. The electrical conductivity and power factor have remarkably increased, while Seebeck coefficient has decreased after the acid treatment when compared to the as prepared CDots/PEDOT:PSS. The optimal electrical conductivity of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films is 1753.92 ± 50 S/cm and the power factor is 148.27 ± 20 μW/mK2 for 10 M of H2SO4 acid at room temperature. The improvement in the thermoelectric performance is ascribed to the impact of high carrier mobility and carrier concentration by the H2SO4 treated nano-composite. A flexible thermoelectric device containing 5 legs is engineered and can generate an output voltage of (V
output = 4.3 mV) at a ΔT = 18 K. Finally, the obtained data confirm that the resultant acid treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS nano-composite represents a great chance for future industrial applications in smart thermoelectric power devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEDOT:PSS
CDots nano-particles
Seebeck effect
Power factor
Thermoelectric materials

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermoelectric property of the treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author kindly thanks Prof. Dr. F. El-Tantawy, Professor of Materials Science, Physics Department, Suez Canal University for his helpful co-operation in measuring UPS spectra and Hall Effect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Shows the electrical conductivity of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films as a function of H2SO4 concentration.
(a) shows the carrier concentration (b) the carrier mobility of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films as a function of H2SO4 concentration.
The AFM images (a) untreated CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films (b) 10 M H2SO4 (c) 12 M H2SO4 (d) 14 M H2SO4.
(a) Raman spectrum of the CDots nanoparticles and (b) Raman spectra of the PEDOT:PSS film, CDots/PEDOT:PSS, 10 M H2SO4 treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS and 12 M H2SO4 treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS film.
Shows the Seebeck coefficient of CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films as a function of H2SO4 concentration.
Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy measurements of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS and H2SO4 treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposites.
Shows the Power factor of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films as a function of H2SO4 concentration.
Shows thermal conductivity of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films as a function of H2SO4 concentration.

(A), shows the Schematic diagram illustrates the thermoelectric power generator. (B) shows the thermoelectric voltage generated by the fabricated generator versus ΔT, (C), shows the output voltage and power as a function of current for the fabricated generator.
Shows the phase separation between PEDOT and PSS when treated with H2SO4 acid.
Shows the thermoelectric parameters of some PEDOT:PSS nanocomposites based on the doping and acid treatment.
Shows different P-Type thermoelectric devices based on PEDOT:PSS Conducting polymer.

row.entry: PEDOT:PSS coated polyester fabric strips connected with silver wire
PEDOT:PSS coated polyester fabric strips connected with Constantan wire

head.ce:title: Acido-treatment of PEDOT:PSS/Carbon Dots (CDots) nano-composite films for high thermoelectric power factor performance and generator

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Thermoelectric materials are considered a main key for sustainable development because they have the ability to convert a low-quality heat into electrical energy. Among them, polymer nano-composite materials have captured attention owing to their unique features such as resilience, low or no toxicity, and abundance. In this work, the thermoelectric parameters of carbon dots/poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulphonate) (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) nano-composite films have been significantly enhanced by using Sulphuric (H2SO4) acids treatment approach. The electrical conductivity and power factor have remarkably increased, while Seebeck coefficient has decreased after the acid treatment when compared to the as prepared CDots/PEDOT:PSS. The optimal electrical conductivity of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films is 1753.92 ± 50 S/cm and the power factor is 148.27 ± 20 μW/mK2 for 10 M of H2SO4 acid at room temperature. The improvement in the thermoelectric performance is ascribed to the impact of high carrier mobility and carrier concentration by the H2SO4 treated nano-composite. A flexible thermoelectric device containing 5 legs is engineered and can generate an output voltage of (V
output = 4.3 mV) at a ΔT = 18 K. Finally, the obtained data confirm that the resultant acid treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS nano-composite represents a great chance for future industrial applications in smart thermoelectric power devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy ranks first among the problems of today's world together with the economic differences, food shortages, and increasing world population, however, we can solve these difficulties if enough sustainable energy has been established [1]. The industrial countries nowadays get most of their energy from fossil fuels. This results in dissipating the energy in the form of the waste heat without getting use of it. For instance, the electricity is the most used form of energy [2]. However, approximately 57% of the fossil fuels that generates the electricity are dissipated as heat energy without use. So, if these lost heat energies have been used to generate some electricity, this will be of great benefit due to saving the consumption of fossil fuels and decreasing the raising in the worldwide temperature. This is the cause why thermoelectric (TE) technology has paid much attention in today's world [1–4].

Recently, thermoelectric materials have captured great attention with the growth of global energy requirements and the environmental problems related to energy harvest and application; due to their features in converting a little amount of heat energy directly into electrical energy. Particularly, the thermoelectric generators which are considered as ''green sources of energy'' because they introduce an effective method to use the heat dissipated to the environment [3,4]. In addition, these generators do not have any moving parts so they produce neither noise nor vibration [3,4]. A dimensionless figure-of-merit (ZT = S2σT/k) parameter was evaluated to measure the performance of thermoelectric materials, where σ is electrical conductivity, S is Seebeck coefficient, T is temperature in Kelvin, and k is thermal conductivity [3,4]. Different inorganic semiconductors and metal oxides (such as Bi2Te3, CaMnO3 and others) have been extensively applied in thermoelectric applications thanks to their high figure of merit value (where the ZTmax for CaMnO3 is 0.5, for Bi2Te3/Sb2Te3 superlattice structure is around 2.6 and ZTmax of 2.6 is obtained for SnSe crystal) [5,6]. Although inorganic materials generally exhibit high performance in thermoelectric devices, these materials are typically expensive and are characterized by brittleness, which renders their application in large areas [7]. Recently, polymer based nano-composites, particularly conducting polymers, are introduced to be a new generation of thermoelectric materials. Because these conducting polymer nano-composites have special features, represented in being inexpensive as well as easy availability of their raw materials, non-toxicity and their lower thermal conductivity as compared to inorganic thermoelectric materials [3–5,8]. Among all conducting polymers, a commercial poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is extensively applied in thermoelectric uses thanks to its durability, economical, visibility, and high processability, but, the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS is very low as compared to inorganic thermoelectric materials. This is due to the oxidization level of PEDOT which is very difficult to be controlled yet, and PEDOT redox state properties that are not stable. Moreover, PEDOT:PSS has a high electrical conductivity but its Seebeck coefficient is still low (14–18 μV/K). So, PEDOT:PSS polymer has been recently doped with carbon materials to improve its thermoelectric performance. From that point, SWNT is embedded into PEDOT:PSS to increase its figure of merit ZT about (0.02) at a 35 wt% of SWNT [9]. Also, the layer-by-layer assembly technique was used to fabricate PEDOT:PSS/PANI/MWCNT nano-composite to obtain a higher Seebeck coefficient (S = 120 μV/K), a high electrical conductivity of 19 × 102 S/cm, and a power factor PF = 2710 μW/mK2 at 80 quad layers [10]. Although the above works reported how to increase the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS, it is limited by the concentration of the carbon materials. Where the highly adjacent carbon materials can make a thermal conductive path and increase the thermal conductivity of the films [3,4] and hence declining the thermoelectric properties. For example, El-Shamy [3] reported on the thermoelectric features of the (CDots/PVA) system and it was found that the thermal conductivity of the (CDots/PVA) system increases with increasing the CDots concentration. The author stated the increase in the thermal conductivity of the (CDots/PVA) system attributed to the increase in the adjacent CDots nanoparticles which make a conductive path and hence increasing the thermal conductivity. Also, similar results have been detected for the CDots/C60/PVA system. It was found that the thermal conductivity of the CDots/C60/PVA system 0.65 W/mK at CDots = 20 wt% [4]. So, to overcome the above limitations, we introduce CDots/PEDOT:PSS films with a high thermoelectric performance with their thermoelectric parameters enhanced by acido-treatment. So, the idea of this work is based on enhancing the thermoelectric power performance of this CDots/PEDOT:PSS film with avoiding (or without) the increase in the concentration of CDots and hence avoiding the increase in thermal conductivity. In fact, many works have been reported in enhancing the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient of the thermoelectric PEDOT:PSS films, and it was found that the only straightforward and efficient way is the post-treatment with chemical organic polar agents and solvents, acids, and small/tiny molecules [11]. From the facts of physics, it is known that the Seebeck coefficient strongly depends on the engineering of the structure of the energy bands and also on the states density of the thermoelectric materials [12]. Adjusting doping levels (carrier's concentration) serves as an effective way to tune the structure of the energy bands and the density of the states of the polymer nano-composite materials for tuning Seebeck coefficient. Many literatures were reported on enhancing the thermoelectric parameters by using post-treatment. For instance, after the post-treatment of the system (PEDOT:PSS), the carrier concentrations increased from (1017–1021 cm−3) for PEDOT:PSS system [13]. Seebeck coefficient for PEDOT:PSS system decreased when the carrier concentration increased due to the increase in its electrical conductivity by four orders [13,14], followed by the increase in power factor and figure of merit. The impact of pH nature on the thermoelectric performance of poly (3, 4 ethylenedioxythiophene)-tosylate (PEDOT:Tos) system was studied by sinking or dipping PEDOT:Tos in aqueous solutions of acid (HCl) or base (NaOH). It was found that at pH ≤ 7, the optimum power factor of 26 μW/mK2 was achieved [15]. The enhancement of the thermoelectric parameters (σ and S) of the PEDOT:PSS system after treating it with (5% DMSO and PEO) was also reported [16]. The obtained thermoelectric parameters were (S = 40 μV/K, σ = 1300 S/cm and PF = 157.35 μW/m K2 at 0.3% of PEO concentration). Ultrafiltration and dedoping are multi-step processes applied to treat the system (PEDOT:PSS) by hydrazine [17]. The obtained thermoelectric parameters (σ = 400–800 S/cm, S = 32–44 μV/K and PF = 115.5 μW/mK2) were recorded.

In fact, there are many stepwise procedures that have been developed to integrate the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient improvements by secondary dopants and tuning the carrier concentration, respectively. However, it has been recognized that the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient are mutually dependent. For example, increasing the carrier concentration can surge the electrical conductivity, but it reduces the Seebeck coefficient, while increasing the mobility of charge carrier can improve them [18]. Methods that can significantly improve the mobility of charges and adequately adjust the doping level (carrier concentration) could be efficacious for maximizing the thermoelectric behavior of polymer system such as PEDOT:PSS, based on the recent advanced experiments.

In order to dominate the doping level and enhance the charge transport, we report here a facile way to remarkably improve the thermoelectric performance of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films through H2SO4 acid treatment. Herein, the system (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) was selected in this work because CDots nano-particles have functional (-OH and –COOH) groups on their surfaces, which can be easily interacted with PEDOT:PSS polymer via strong interfacial interaction (π-π conjugated interaction) that can stimulate the thermoelectric performance. Also, H2SO4 acid was selected here to treat PEDOT:PSS, where H2SO4 makes a change in the conformation of PEDOT chains and also boosts the elimination of some PSSH chains, though the reaction PSS− +H2SO4 → PSSH +
H S
O 4 -
where
H S
O 4 -
ions can replace PSS− producing a reduction in the width of energy barrier of charge hopping of the intra/inter chains, and the change in the conformation of PEDOT produces a more delocalization of charge carriers and hence the thermoelectric parameters. It was found that the electrical conductivity and power factor have remarkably increased, while Seebeck coefficient has decreased after the acid treatment when compared to the as prepared CDots/PEDOT:PSS. The optimal electrical conductivity of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films is 1753.92 ± 50 S/cm and the power factor is 148.27 ± 20 μW/mK2 for 10 M of H2SO4 acid at room temperature. Also, a flexible thermoelectric device containing 5 legs is engineered and can generate an output voltage of (V
output = 4.3 mV) at a ΔT = 18 K.

PEDOT:PSS aqueous solution (CLEVIOS PH1000) was purchased from Aldrich Chemicals. 2 wt% was the concentration of PEDOT:PSS. d-glucose (99.8% purity), acetone (99.9%) and Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) were obtained from the Al-Nasr Chemicals company, Egypt.
Microwave assisted hydrothermal heating method was used to create CDots nano-particles [3,4,19–30], see supplementary materials S1.

The solution casting method was used to create the treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS nano-composite films [3,4,19–30]. CDots nano-particles (10 wt%) were added to PEDOT:PSS solution and stirred for 20 ± 5 min. After that, CDots/PEDOT:PSS solutions were dropped into glass substrates of (2 × 2 cm2) with spin coating (2000 rpm for 1 min) and dried at 45 ± 3 °C for 24 h to obtained CDots/PEDOT:PSS films. The treated films were prepared by the same method through adding H2SO4 acid with different concentrations (2–14 M) to the CDots/PEDOT:PSS solutions. Before the fabrication process, the glass substrates were cleaned with a mixture solution of acetone, deionized water, and isopropyl alcohol.

The said films were cut into several strips with dimension (20 × 2 mm) and located on a PET film (10 mm was the distance between each two strips). The film strips were attached to each other by using silver paste and silver wires to obtain a good electrical connection. The output power (Poutput) of the device is measured with (2182A/2450) Keithley apparatus and rheostat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermoelectric property of the treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films

ce:section.ce:para: Although the increasing number of literatures on enhancing the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films, their composites with carbon materials and their treatments by acidic or basic agents (Table 1 ), they are still low for high thermoelectric performance applications. So, in this work we introduce a trail to enhance the thermoelectric performance of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films by using the acido-treatment with H2SO4. In this study, the impact of H2SO4 acid on the electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, thermal conductivity and power factor was briefly studied.

Fig (1) shows the electrical conductivity of the untreated and treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films versus the concentration of H2SO4 acid. As seen, the electrical conductivity value of untreated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films was measured and it was found to be 632.6 ± 30 S/cm. The high value of conductivity is attributed to the creation of a conductive path from CDots in PEDOT:PSS matrix [3,4,19–30]. This conductive path enables the generation of a charge transfer complexs between CDots and PEDOT:PSS segments. This leads to increasing the charge carrier concentration and carrier mobility in the nano-composite and hence increases the electrical conductivity of the nano-composite. However, when the CDots/PEDOT:PSS treated with 2 M of H2SO4 acid the electrical conductivity increases to be 885.7 ± 15 S/cm. This is attributed to the ionic conductivity produced from the introduction of H2SO4 acid, where H2SO4 boosts the elimination of some PSSH chains, though the reaction PSS− +H2SO4 → PSSH +
H S
O 4 -
where
H S
O 4 -
ions can replace PSS− producing a reduction in the width of energy barrier of charge hopping of the intra/inter chains, and leads to reducing the interactions between PEDOT and PSS. This leads to easier removal of PSS, higher carrier mobility and higher electrical conductivity [36–38]. By increasing the concentration of H2SO4 acid the electrical conductivity of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS films significantly increased by 3 times than the untreated CDots/PEDOT:PSS and reached to 1753.92 ± 50 S/cm with H2SO4 acid (10 M).

Also, from the figure it can be seen that the increase in electrical conductivity (σ) values of the acid treated films occurs with increasing the concentration of H2SO4 acid until reaches 10 M of H2SO4 acid then decreases. The enhancement of the electrical conductivity of the treated (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) films can be explained basically by increasing the mobility of carrier's and the carrier concentration in the nano-composite films after the acid treatment [39]. From the physical facts, the electrical conductivity can be stated by equation:
σ = n e μ
Where the concentration of carriers is represented by (n), the charge of electrons is represented by (e) and the mobility of carriers is represented by (μ). The increase in both the carrier concentration (1019–1020 cm3) and the mobility of carriers (2.31–9.02 cm2/V s) from (2–10 M of H2SO4) lead to increasing the electrical conductivity. This is due to after removal the PSS, the excess of
H S
O 4 -
ions will fill the vacancies in the films and links the PEDOT chains producing a high carrier concentration and high carrier mobility and hence increasing the electrical conductivity. This explanation is supported by the measurement of both the concentration and the mobility of carriers (Fig. 2 a and b). As seen, both the carrier concentration and the carrier mobility increase gradually with increasing the concentration of H2SO4 acid, which relates to the increase in electrical conductivities of the films. Two possible reasons were beyond increasing the carrier concentration and carrier mobility with increasing H2SO4 acid concentration from (2–10 M). The 1st reason is the increase in the interfacial adhesion between H2SO4 and polymer matrix, which accelerated the driving force of the transport of the charge carriers. While the 2nd reason becomes obvious when increasing H2SO4,
H S
O 4 -
ions replace PSS and fill the vacant volume in the conductive sites; this produces an increase in the hopping or tunneling of the charge carriers and increases the density of electrical mesh and/or CDots particles interconnects within the polymer matrix. This is strong evidence that the addition of
H S
O 4 -
ions into the polymer acts as a carrier reservoir of the entire composite.

On the other hand, the reduction in the electrical conductivity beyond 10 M H2SO4 acid may be attributed to (i) the decrease in the carrier mobility beyond 10 M H2SO4 acid (8.60 cm2/V s at 12 M of H2SO4) leading to a decrease in the electrical conductivity. (ii) Also, the treated (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) films by acid can produce a phase separation between PEDOT:PSS and CDots nano-particles [40]. Definitely, when the films were treated with acid, the acid dissociated producing H+ protons that can bind with PSS− groups of PEDOT:PSS creating weakness in the Columbic interaction between PEDOT:PSS and CDots, producing a phase separation, which in turn results in a reduction in the ionic transport and hence decreasing the electrical conductivity beyond (10 M H2SO4). This explanation is supported by the study of the surface morphology by AFM microscope (Fig. 3 a–d).

Fig. 3, shows the surface morphology of the untreated and treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films. For CDots/PEDOT:PSS films (Fig. 3a), the image shows a rough surface with some defects and cracks due to embedding CDots into PEDOT:PSS. The mean root square Rrms value of the surface is 1.51 nm. But when treating CDots/PEDOT:PSS film with 10 M H2SO4 (Fig. 3b), it is clear that the surface becomes smooth and uniform (Rrms = 1.16 nm), because when treating PEDOT:PSS/CDots films with H2SO4 acid, the
S
O 4
2 -
ions fill and occupy the vacancies and defects on the films and links the chains with each other and hence increasing the electrical conductivity [3,4,19–30]. However, after treating the films with acid higher than 10 M H2SO4 (Fig. 3c and d), the phase separation was clearly observed on the surface of the films. A larger domain size was observed and the film becomes rougher (Rrms = 1.92 and 2.43 nm for 12 and 14 M H2SO4). The observed change in films morphology (AFM images) and the obtained Rrms values together show the phase separation between PEDOT:PSS chains and CDots. This new structure (phase separation) obstructs the electrical contact between PEDOT:PSS chains and CDots, which in turn hinders the carrier mobility, hence decreasing the electrical conductivity at high H2SO4 concentrations (12 and 14 M).

More confirmation has been performed by measuring the Raman spectra (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a shows the Raman spectrum of the CDots. As seen, two bands have been detected at 1353 and 1595 cm−1, these bands are ascribed to the D and G bands of the CDots nanoparticles [3,4,19–30]. The vibrations create the D bands are ascribed to the (C) atom attached to dangling bonds and the vibrations create the G bands are ascribed to the sp2 hybridization of (C) atom. Fig. 4b shows the Raman spectrum of the pure PEDOT:PSS film. As seen, there are four bands at 1258, 1365, 1435 and 1589 cm−1, these bands are ascribed to inter-ring C–C stretching vibrations, stretching C–C vibrations, symmetric CC stretching vibrations and antisymmetric CC stretching vibrations [3,4,14]. Also, it can be seen that the positions and intensities of these bands are shifted toward the higher wavenumber when CDots doped PEDOT:PSS and after treating the CDots/PEDOT:PSS with 10 M H2SO4 acid, this is attributed to the strong interaction between PEDOT:PSS and CDots and between CDots/PEDOT:PSS and H2SO4 acid. As observed, the band at 1365 cm−1 (D band) for the 12 M H2SO4 acid treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS shifted to higher wavenumber as compared to the other films. However, the band at 1589 cm−1 (G band) shifted to a lower wavenumber as compared to the other films. This refers to the strong interaction between the H2SO4 acid and unconjugated PSS. Also, the band at 1435 cm−1 shifted to a higher wavenumber, this is attributed to the strong interaction (π-π conjugated type) between the CDots (enrich with electrons) and aromatic PEDOT structure and between the H2SO4 acid and the CDots/PEDOT:PSS [3,4,14]. These observations prove that the strong interaction between both CDots and H2SO4 acid and PEDOT and/or PSS chains which enhances the phase separation of PEDOT:PSS [3,4,14].

To better understand the chemistry of the phase separation and the electronic transport properties, a similar experiment has been parallely performed only for PEDOT:PSS system. In this experiment the impact of H2SO4 on the electrical conductivity and the surface morphology of the PEDOT:PSS was studied (Supplementary Materials S4).

As seen (Fig. S1), shows the impact of H2SO4 acid concentration on the electrical conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS films. As seen, the electrical conductivity increases with increasing the H2SO4 concentration from (2–10 M) then decreases. The reason behind this behavior is the phase separation between PEDOT:PSS chains for higher H2SO4 concentration. This behavior supported by measuring the surface morphology of the films by AFM (Fig. S2). In fact, the elimination of insulating PSS component is useful to increase PEDOT-rich chains, and this leads to generating of highly conductive PEDOT with 3D conducting networks. Thus, charge carriers can transport further through the conducting networks, i.e. the charge hopping between inter- and intra-chains becomes easier, which is useful to improve electrical conductivity of the composite films [41]. H2SO4 used as both a proton acceptor and a proton donor in the procedure of acid treatment [41]. Some of the PSS− ions will be neutralized by the protons of H2SO4 upon acid treatment, producing the disappearance of Coulombic attraction between PEDOT and PSSH as presented in the following equations [36].
(1)
H 2
S
O 4
→
H +
+ H S
O 4 -
p
a l
k
= - 6 . 4
(2)
P S S H → P S
S -
+
H +
p
a l
k
= - 2 . 8

As seen, the H2SO4 has
p
a l
k
higher than
p a k
of PSSH, Thus PSS− units get protonated upon treatment with H2SO4. Hence, the whole reaction can be stated as:
(3)
H 2
S
O 4
+ P S S → H S
O 4 -
+ P S S H

PSS units get protonated with H2SO4, and the coulomb interactions between PEDOT and PSS become weaker (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, a part of PSS− replaced by
H S
O 4 -
or
S
O 4
2 -
as the counter-ion in the composite films. Neutral hydrophilic PSSH units can't display Coulombic attractions with hydrophobic PEDOT grains, producing the phase separation/segregation after 10 M H2SO4.

The Seebeck coefficient of the treated (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) films as a function of H2SO4 acid concentration is described in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the Seebeck coefficient of the treated films reduces with the growth of H2SO4 acid concentration at room temperature. Hot (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) side will drive high thermal velocity electrons that diffuse to the cold (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) side with lower thermal velocity electrons when conducting different temperatures to the sides (CDots/PEDOT:PSS), thus producing an alteration in the charge carrier densities. As a result, the electric voltage was generated between the sides of (CDots/PEDOT:PSS). From the obtained data, a positive value of the Seebeck coefficient in the entire temperature ranges indicates p-type of the treated (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) [3,4]. It is well known that the Seebeck coefficient is the thermo-voltage produced in a material when applied to a temperature difference between its ends and depends on the entropy of charge carriers inside this material [42]. For polymer systems, as a result of their disorder property (amorphous nature), the Seebeck coefficient can be stated from the relation [43]:
S =
K B
e
(
E -
E f
K B
T
)
, where (EF) is the Fermi level, E is the energy of the localized states, e is the electronic charge and KB is the Boltzmann constant. As the carrier concentration increases (2.50 × 1019 for 2 M to 3.32 × 1020 cm−3 for 10 M), this will push Fermi level (EF) towards the conduction band. This produces a decrease in Seebeck coefficient value [3,4,44]. As seen the Seebeck coefficient still decreases with increasing the molarity above 10 M H2SO4, this due to the carrier concentration still surges from (3.37 × 1020 for 12 M to 3.51 × 1020 cm−3 for 14 M). So, the growth of the carrier concentration can explain the reduction in the Seebeck coefficient value, which agrees with the previous reports [13,44,45].

In fact, the Seebeck coefficient is based on the slope of the density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level according to Mott's formula,
S =
π 2
K B
T
3 e
{
1 n
d n
( E )
d E
+
1 μ
d μ
( E )
d E
}
E =
E f
, in which n = carrier density, E
F = the Fermi level and μ = carrier mobility [46,47]. Also, many papers recorded that the treatment of the polymer systems with the acids produces a slightly lower work function of the system and hence E F is pushed away from the valence band, where the density of states changes abruptly. Also the degree of crystallinity (degrees of order) will have an effect on the density of states. With energy disorder, the density of states broadens and softens at the edge of the band; as a result, the slope of the density of states at Fermi levels (EF) reduces and the Seebeck coefficient value is low for amorphous polymer. This agrees with the facts of the Seebeck coefficient value in amorphous or semi-crystalline polymers is smaller/lower than semiconductors with crystalline nature [48–50]. This explanation is supported with UPS spectra (Fig. 6 ). As seen from the figure, the nano-composite films treated with acid have a work function (ϕ) lower than the untreated films. The work functions of the nano-composite films were calculated from a UPS spectrum using the equation, ϕ = hν – E
0
– E
F , where the work function is ϕ, the source energy is hν (21.22 eV for He I photons), the secondary electron cut off is E
0 and the Fermi energy is E
F. The calculated work function for the treated films was found to be (4.21 eV) lower than the untreated films (5.22 eV). Thus the lower (ϕ) indicates the moving of E
F away from the valence band, where the density of states changes abruptly, thus reducing the Seebeck coefficient [34,47].

Fig. 7 shows the power factor (PF) of the acid treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films as a function of H2SO4 acid concentration. As seen, the power factor (PF) of the acid treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films surges with increasing H2SO4 acid concentration from (2–10 M). It is known that the power factor depends strongly upon both the Seebeck coefficient and the electrical conductivity of the films, (
P =
S 2
σ
). Although the Seebeck coefficient has slightly changed, the electrical conductivity increases gradually with increasing H2SO4 acid concentration (2–10 M) due to the increase in the carrier concentration, which leads to an increase in the power factor. Also, it was found that the power factor decreases after 10 M of H2SO4. This behavior can be illustrated on the basis of the carrier mobility. In fact, the power factor (PF) can be obtained from the
P =
S 2
σ
by breaking down into
P =
(
−
K B
e
log
P
N μ
)
2
μ
e p
[51]. Where KB is the Boltzmann constant, e is the electronic charge,
N μ
is the effective density of states of transport level, p is the carrier concentration of hole and carrier mobility μ
. From the equation it can be seen that the power factor is strongly depends on the carrier mobility μ . As seen the power factor decreases with increasing the H2SO4 concentration after 10 M, this is due to the decrease in the carrier mobility after 10 M.

As seen, the highest power factor obtained in this work was 148.27 ± 20 μW/mK2 for the concentration 10 M H2SO4 acid, which is higher than that of the untreated (CDots/PEDOT:PSS) films (116.2 μW/mK2). This work also provides nano-composite films with power factor value larger than that of the system PEDOT:PSS/PC-Te (51.4 μW/mK2) [52]; Te–Cu1.75Te/PEDOT:PSS films (84 μW/mK2) [53] and hybrids Te–Bi2Te3/PEDOT:PSS films (60.05 μW/mK2) [54]. But it is still smaller than those of the hybrid inorganic–organic TiS2(HA)0.08(H2O)0.22(DMSO)0.03 superlattice (450 μW/mK2 [55]) and the TiS2(HA)0.19(DMSO)0.35 and TiS2(HA)0.25 films (904 μW/mK2 at 300 K [56]).

Fig. 8 shows the output of plane thermal conductivity of all films. It is known that the thermal conductivity (κ) is a contribution of two components, electron transporting heat (electron thermal conductivity)
k
e l e c t r o n
and the phonons travelling through the lattices (phonon thermal conductivity)
k
p h o n o n
[3,4].
k
t o t a l
=
k
e l e c t r o n
+
k
p h o n o n

As seen, the thermal conductivity of the untreated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films increases just when inserting CDots. This suggests that the phenomenon of phonon transport is less influenced by the localized CDots agglomeration. The cause behind the higher thermal connectivity of CDots/PEDOT:PSS films is the phonon scattering effect of nano-interfaces between CDots and PEDOT:PSS [3,4,56]. Where CDots produces conductive paths in the PEDOT:PSS matrix and increases the network density inside the matrix. This leads to a decrease in the thermal resistance and indicates free path of phonons. This leads to increasing the phonon thermal conductivity (
k
p h o n o n
) and hence, the thermal conductivity increases. Similar behavior was reported in Ref. [3,4,57]. Besides that, the electron thermal conductivity (
k
e l e c t r o n
) contributes in the total thermal conductivity due to the insertion of CDots which produces an increase in the charge carrier concentration and hence increasing the k
total of the CDots/PEDOT:PSS (see Fig. 2a).

After H2SO4 acid treatment, all films displayed a significant increase in the values of thermal conductivity with increasing H2SO4 acid concentration. This can be attributing to the increase in the electronic thermal conductivity (
k
e l e c t r o n
) due to the increase in the charge carrier concentration by increasing H2SO4 acid concentration. Besides, the contribution of the phonon thermal conductivity from CDots/PEDOT:PSS. As seen, there is an increase in both the electronic thermal conductivity and the phonon thermal conductivity. Therefore, the thermal conductivity κ increases. Although the increase in the thermal conductivity of the treated CDots/PEDOT:PSS films but it still shows small values of thermal conductivity, which give these nano-composites the opportunity to be a good thermoelectric materials.
Thermoelectric generator was engineered by fixing 5 legs of thermoelectric material in a series connection by using silver wires and silver paste; see (Fig. 9 a).

Fig. 9b represents the generated output thermo-voltage (V) from the engineered device versus ΔT. It is clear that the output thermo-voltage (V) surges with the increase in ΔT due to the Seebeck effect. Also, the output thermo-voltage (V) surges with the increase in the number of thermoelectric legs, when the device involves 5 legs, the output thermo-voltage (V) reaches 4.3 mV at ΔT = 18 K. It is worthy to mention that there are variances between the output voltage (3.4 mV) at applied ΔT = 18 K, and the value of the calculated output voltage according to the relation:
V = Δ T × S × N
, where the Seebeck coefficient is denoted by S, and the number of the strips is denoted by N. This may be attributed to the heat loss caused by the Ohmic contact between the wires and the strips, as well as between the Ag paste and the strips due to the phase separation. Similar behavior was reported in Refs. [45]. However, the obtained data shows the possibility of improving thermoelectric legs to improve the performance of thermoelectric device. Fig. 9c shows the generated voltage/power of the best performing thermoelectric sample tested (open-circuit voltage V oc = 4.3 mV, and short-circuit current I sc = 8.6 μA) plotted as a function of the current. It is clear that, the experimental output power density (~3.9 × 10−3 nW/cm2) was obtained at the steady-state temperature difference ΔT = 18 K. In fact, it is need to 1.5 V at a minimum value of the output voltage for any small wearable device to work. However, the obtained output voltage (4.3 mV) is acceptable when compared to other reported thermoelectric devices (Table 2 ). The output voltage (4.3 mV) in this work is obtained at a small ΔT = 18 K and five thermoelectric legs, which can be increased by increasing the temperature difference ΔT and/or by increasing the number of legs. Moreover, the output power Poutput can be further improved through adapting the structure of the device, increasing the number of legs and/or combining flexible materials of N-type with high thermoelectric performance to assemble P–N type thermoelectric one.
Ahmed Gamal El-Shamy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author kindly thanks Prof. Dr. F. El-Tantawy, Professor of Materials Science, Physics Department, Suez Canal University for his helpful co-operation in measuring UPS spectra and Hall Effect.
